summary: Hotz reflects on starting tinygrad and his refusal to play the typical startup game of hype over substance. He uses Cruise Automation as a cautionary example of a company that survives by selling perception rather than delivering products, burning $5M daily while promising revenue is 'right around the corner.' He outlines the disgusting but effective playbook: hire aggressively, market before you have a product, sell vaporware, raise more money based on the appearance of success. Despite knowing this playbook works, he refuses to follow it, preferring truth over success.
key_points: Cruise Automation exemplifies how companies survive by selling perception rather than delivering results, losing $5M/day while promising imminent revenue
The startup success playbook involves treating the company itself as the product: hire fast, get fancy offices, run ads, sell before building
Communication in business is often about achieving desired outcomes rather than conveying truth
Value destruction (consuming resources) may matter more than capital efficiency as a measure of company impact
Hotz would rather fail than compromise his integrity by playing the hype game
money_quotes: The point of communication is not to convey truth, it's to acheive desired outcomes.
I know what it takes to succeed, but I'm not willing to do it.
Don't have a product? It doesn't matter! The company is your product.
Nobody is driving in a Zoox, Zoox's aren't real. But that doesn't matter, use photoshop. Perception is reality.
It's only other people's time you are wasting, it doesn't cost you much. You can build a product later.
If you lie to the world you lie to yourself and one day you can no longer tell what's true or not.
I would live under a bridge and eat out of dumpsters if I got to know the truth.
I would rather lose than sell out.
key_insight: The startup world rewards perception over reality, and while Hotz understands this playbook perfectly, he considers following it a form of self-destruction worse than failure.
